In English, prosodic units such as syllable and foot are
referred to in the phonological description. Here we are going
to refer to foot, which is trochaic in English as in (1).
di{ňąsaster} sy{ňąnopsis} mi{ňąmosa} (1) a.

Now take a look at the data in (2). Voiceless stops are
aspirated when they are followed by a stressed vowel,
whether it is a primary stress as in (2a) or a secondary stress as
in (2b). But even before a stressed vowel, they are not
aspirated when it is preceded by /s/ as in (2c). Lastly, they are
not aspirated when they are followed by an unstressed vowel
as in (2d). So the phonological description of the aspiration
phenomenon must be complicated without referring to foot.
